source_item.title: PM Narendra Modi launches India's first hydrogen train: what it really changes

slide1_hook.label: India's First Hydrogen Train Just Changed History
slide1_hook.subhead: Biggest green rail milestone since electrification. Here's what it means.

slide2_backstory.label: THE BACKSTORY
slide2_backstory.headline: India's Rail Was Nearly All Green
slide2_backstory.body: Indian Railways has electrified over 99% of its network, but heritage hill routes like Darjeeling still ran on diesel. In 2023, Railways announced plans for 35 hydrogen trains, and picked Jind-Sonipat in Haryana as the test route.

slide3_news.label: THE UPDATE
slide3_news.headline: NaMo Green Rail Is Now Officially Running
slide3_news.body: On July 17, PM Modi flagged off India's first hydrogen train, the NaMo Green Rail, from Jind in Haryana. Built entirely in India, it seats 2,600 passengers, runs the 89 km Jind-Sonipat route, and emits only water vapour.

slide4_why.label: WHY IT MATTERS
slide4_why.headline: Historic Milestone, But Real Tests Ahead
bullets: Joins Germany and China with hydrogen trains in service
Plans 35 more trains for routes like Darjeeling and Nilgiri
Fuel cells are imported, and green hydrogen costs more
Germany's hydrogen train fleet struggled with cell wear

slide5_engage.label: YOUR TAKE
slide5_engage.headline: India built the train body but imports the fuel cell, its core engine. Is this really 'Make in India', or are we calling a stepping stone the finish line?
slide5_engage.cta: Follow for more on India's biggest business and infrastructure stories, explained simply. 🚆
